rationale_for_baseline_tier: Active upper gastrointestinal haemorrhage (melaena) with haemodynamic compromise — orthostatic hypotension, tachycardia, pallor, diaphoresis and presyncope indicating compensated hypovolaemic shock. This is an immediate threat to life; the source clinician placed the patient nil by mouth, sited large-bore IV access, commenced fluids and called 000 for priority ambulance transport. The patient can self-report genuine time-critical features (melaena, near-collapse on standing), so T5 (call 000) is the correct tier — not merely a T3 telehealth uplift ceiling. Self-transport is unsafe given the fainting risk.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Vomiting fresh red blood or coffee-ground material
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Immediately — update the 000 dispatcher to upgrade priority
escalation_edges.rationale: Haematemesis signals brisk ongoing upper GI bleeding and imminent decompensation.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If he vomits blood or something that looks like coffee grounds, call 000 straight back and tell them the bleeding has got worse so they upgrade the ambulance.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Collapse, fainting, or loss of consciousness
escalation_edges.rationale: Syncope indicates decompensated haemorrhagic shock; lay him flat with legs raised and keep 000 on the line.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If he faints or you can't wake him properly, lay him flat, raise his legs, keep him warm and tell 000 immediately — start CPR if he stops breathing normally and you know how.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Worsening dizziness, confusion or increasing weakness before the ambulance arrives
escalation_edges.rationale: Progressive symptoms mean ongoing blood loss; the emergency response must be escalated, not delayed.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If he gets more dizzy, confused or weaker while you wait, call 000 back and let them know he's getting worse.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Spouse is present and made the call and can act on instructions
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Direct the spouse to unlock the door for paramedics, keep the patient flat and nil by mouth, remove the naproxen, and watch for and report vomiting blood or collapse to 000.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Daily NSAID use continuing at home
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Explicitly instruct that all anti-inflammatories are stopped now; the open naproxen bottle should be put away.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: Call 000 for an ambulance right now and tell them he is passing black tarry stools, feels very weak and faint when standing, and may be bleeding internally from his stomach. Keep him lying flat — do not let him stand or walk. Give him nothing to eat or drink. Stop the Naproxen. Stay with him and unlock the front door for the paramedics.
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: 000 is available 24 hours a day — this is an emergency and must not wait for clinic hours or a GP appointment.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Hospital will provide a discharge summary and endoscopy report; the GP should follow up H. pylori eradication, confirm the NSAID has been stopped, and arrange safe alternative analgesia with gastroprotection.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Treating this as a chronic dyspepsia / H. pylori flare and advising self-care, antacids or a routine/urgent GP review instead of emergency escalation.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: Ongoing haemorrhage progresses to decompensated shock, exsanguination and death while the patient waits at home.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Advising the patient to make their own way to the emergency department rather than calling 000, despite orthostatic instability — syncope and collapse en route with no medical support and delayed resuscitation.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Over-triage is not a meaningful risk here — T5 is the correct tier for an active bleed with haemodynamic compromise.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: The exact bleeding source (peptic ulcer vs malignancy vs varices) and the volume of blood lost cannot be determined via telehealth.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Recognise the emergency pattern and escalate to 000 immediately; the source is confirmed in hospital by endoscopy. Certainty about the lesion is not required to act.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Delaying escalation to 'confirm' the diagnosis remotely, or downgrading to a home trial of acid suppression because the source is unproven.
